content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 22, 1991

Vincent F. Biondo, Jr.
City Attorney
City of Carlsbad
1200 Carlsbad Village Drive
Carlsbad, CA 92008-1989

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-054

Dear Mr. Biondo:

You have requested advice concerning application of the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act") to the duties of Carlsbad City Councilmember Margaret
Stanton. She is also a member of the Carlsbad Redevelopment Com-
mission. The following advice is based upon the facts provided in
your letter and our telephone conversation on March 22, 1991.

QUESTION

Under the Act, may Ms. Stanton participate in certain
projects pending before the redevelopment commission?

CONCLUSION

Ms. Stanton may participate in projects pending before the
redevelopment commission if there will not be a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on either Jazzercise, Inc.
or on her real property interest.

FACTS

Ms. Stanton is an officer and director of Jazzercise, Inc.
She is the general manager of the company and an employee of the
corporation. She does not have any ownership interest in the

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

File No. A-91-054
Page 2


corporation, which is closely-held and wholly-owned by the Missett
Family Trust.

     Jazzercise operates approximately 4,500 franchises worldwide.
Its revenues from the franchises, a mail order and retail store
and its television operation are approximately $15 million annu-
ally. You stated that the pretax income of Jazzercise is ap-
proximately $1 million, the net income exceeds $400,000 and the
tangible assets are approximately $2 million.

     The corporate office is located at 2808 Roosevelt Street,
which is in the redevelopment area of Carlsbad. The corporation
signed a five year lease for this office in October 1990. The
corporation operates a mail order and retail store and a televi-
sion production company at this location. It also subleases a
part of the offices to several of its local franchisees for dance
fitness classes. These franchisees pay Jazzercise 35 percent of
the gross student fees collected per month as rent and 20 percent
of the gross student fees collected as a continuing franchise fee.

     The City of Carlsbad adopted a redevelopment plan in 1981. It
includes six subareas with specific goals and desired land uses.
The Jazzercise corporate offices are located within subarea 5,
which is planned as the major tourist and related commercial
center for the project area. The following redevelopment projects
are pending and will go to the redevelopment commission for a vote
in the near future:

     1.  A parking plan, including financing sources, which
identifies how parking needs for the village redevelopment area
may be met for future projects. Preparation of the parking plan
was funded by the redevelopment tax increment and benefits the
entire project area. Implementation may involve the imposition of
additional fees on property owners located within the area to
finance the necessary improvements. It is also possible a
surcharge will be added to the business license tax.

     2.  The redevelopment commission is completing a major
streetscape project funded by tax allocation bonds. A fifth phase
of this project consisting of street improvements, decorative pav-
ers, storm drains, traffic signals, street furniture, art work and
street lighting is ready for approval. Phase II will cost ap-
proximately $1.2 million. It will be located on Carlsbad Village
Drive (Elm Avenue) from Washington Street to Pico Pico. Traffic
signals will be installed along Grand Avenue. The project is
located 500 to 1,000 feet from the Jazzercise corporate offices.

     3.  The final design of a rapid rail transit center is being
proposed and funded by the County of San Diego and the North
County Transit District, a separate legal entity. The redevelop-
ment commission has signed a cooperative agreement with both agen-
cies which requires the city's approval of the final design. The
project will include a transit center structure, parking
facilities and lighting. A bus transfer facility west of the

File No. A-91-054
Page 3


railroad tracks will be constructed requiring relocation of an
existing lumber business on State Street. The rapid rail transit
center station site will be bounded on three sides by Carlsbad
Village Drive (Elm Avenue), State Street and Grand Avenue. It is
approximately 1,000 feet from the Jazzercise office.

     4.  The redevelopment commission staff is preparing a master
plan to determine how the redevelopment area will develop in the
next ten to twenty years. Elements of the plan will include, but
are not limited to, the design, land use, historic preservation
and cultural arts, and financing of projects in the redevelopment
area. The plan will be funded from the redevelopment tax incre-
ment.

     5.  The city council has authorized the study and possible
formation of a second redevelopment project area generally located
along the Pacific Ocean and to the south of the existing
redevelopment area. The closest point of the proposed second
redevelopment area is 2,000 feet from the Jazzercise corporate
offices. The outer boundary is 6.5 miles away. It is also
located approximately 2,000 feet from Ms. Stanton's personal
residence. The next action will be appointment of the project
area committee and designation of the new project area.

     6.  The design review board within the redevelopment agency
makes recommendations to the agency on specific projects. This
board has recommended approval of a $3 million private development
project which would allow the construction of a three story,
28,817 square foot mixed-use office and retail building, located
at the southeast corner of State Street and Laguna Drive ap-
proximately 2,000 feet from the Jazzercise corporate offices.

     7.  An application has been submitted for a 3,100 square foot
retail project, costing $150,000 and located at 2933 Roosevelt
Street, 250 feet from the Jazzercise corporate offices.

     8.  Another application is pending for a $25,000 project at
2558 Roosevelt Street, 500 feet from the Jazzercise offices. It
consists of an intensification of the existing general office use
on the site to medical use and requires parking provisions on an
enjoining lot by private lease.

     9.  Within the redevelopment area there is an Army/Navy
academy located at the northwest corner of Cypress Avenue and
Carlsbad Boulevard. This nonprofit organization is preparing for
a total renovation of its campus and has proposed a $20 million
construction project, which is located 1,000 feet from the
Jazzercise corporate offices.

     10.  A $2.5 million senior housing project is proposed at the
southwest corner of Carlsbad Boulevard and Beech Avenue, which is
located from 1,000 to 2,000 feet from the Jazzercise offices.

File No. A-91-054
Page 4


11.  A mixed-use retail and a condominium apartment complex
with 20 housing units is proposed for the southeast corner of
Carlsbad Boulevard and Beech Avenue, 750 to 2,000 feet from the
Jazzercise offices. The project will cost approximately $15.5
million and may require financial assistance from the redevelop-
ment agency.

                              ANALYSIS

     The Act prohibits a public official from making, participat-
ing in, or using her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which she knows or has reason to know she has a
financial interest. (Section 87100.) A public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of her immediate family or on, among other
things:

          (a)  Any business entity in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect investment worth
     one thousand dollars ($1000) or more.

          (b)  Any real property in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect interest worth
     one thousand dollars ($1000) or more.

          (c)  Any source of income, other than gifts
     and other than loans by a commercial lending
     institution in the regular course of business on
     terms available to the public without regard to
     official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dol-
     lars ($250) or more in value provided to, received
     by or promised to the public official within 12
     months prior to the time when the decision is made.

          (d)  Any business entity in which the public
     official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
     employee, or holds any position of management.

          (e)  Any donor of, or any intermediary or
     agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
     two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
     provided to, received by, or promised to the public
     official within 12 months prior to the time when
     the decision is made.

                         Section 87103(a)-(e).

     As a public official, Ms. Stanton may not participate in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on any source of
income of over $250 within 12 months prior to the decision, on a

File No. A-91-054
Page 5


business entity in which she is an officer or director, or on any
real property interest. (Section 87103(a), (b), and (e).)

Foreseeability

The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur. To be foreseeable, the effects of a decision must be more than a mere possibility; however, certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v. Downey
Community Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 980-991;
Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817, 822; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198 (copy enclosed).) The Act seeks to prevent more
than actual conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow,
supra, at 823.)

The purpose of any redevelopment plan is to promote sound
development and redevelopment of blighted areas. The anticipated
result of redevelopment is an increase in property values and an
improved business climate within the project area, which benefits
the community as a whole. The very nature of redevelopment
projects has led the Commission to find that it is reasonably
foreseeable that there will be a financial effect on real property
values and business interests located within or near project
areas. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, Advice Letters to
Haight, No. A-81-509, and Phillips, No. A-87-166, copies enclosed.
See also Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Com., supra,
at 983.)

In addition, the location of the various redevelopment
projects within a short distance of the Jazzercise corporate offices, where part of the office space is leased to local
franchises for dance fitness classes, may also have a foreseeable
financial effect on the Jazzercise business. (See In re Gillmor
(1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 38, bottom of page 41, copy enclosed.)

Materiality

Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official's economic interest in a decision is "materially" affected as required by Section 87103. If the official's
economic interest is directly involved in the decision, Regulation
18702.1 applies to determine materiality. However, if the
official's economic interest is indirectly affected by the decision, Regulations 18702.2 to 18702.6 apply to determine whether
the effect of the decision is material.

Ms. Stanton is an officer, director, general manager and
employee of Jazzercise, Inc. Jazzercise may be indirectly affected by certain decisions regarding future projects in or near
the redevelopment area. Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed)
provides guidelines to ascertain whether the effect of a decision
on a business entity indirectly involved in a decision is material. You have stated that the financial standards in Regulation

File No. A-91-054
Page 6


18702.2(f) apply to Jazzercise. Under this regulation, the effect
of a decision is material as to a business entity in which the
official has an economic interest if:

(f) For any business entity not covered by
subdivisions (a) or (b) which meets the financial
standards for listing on the National Association
of Securities Dealers National Market List, the
tests in subdivision (c) may be applied. The
standards are as follows: The business entity has
net tangible assets of at least $4,000,000, and had
pre-tax income for the last fiscal year of at
$750,000, with net income from that period of at
least $400,000.

The tests in subdivision (c) are:

(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $30,000 or more; or

(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $7,500 or more; or

(3) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $30,000 or more.


Therefore, you must apply the tests in subdivision (c) to
each redevelopment project and decision enumerated above to
determine if there will be a material financial effect on
Jazzercise. If Jazzercise is materially affected, Ms. Stanton may
not participate in either the city council or redevelopment com-
mission decisions.

Project number 5 discusses the possible formation of a second
redevelopment project which is approximately 2,000 feet from Ms.
Stanton's personal residence. Pursuant to Regulation
18702.3(a)(3)(A) (copy enclosed), if the decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of $10,000 or more on the
fair market value of Ms. Stanton's property, she may not
participate in the decision.

Public Generally

Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if the
effect if distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87103.) For the city council's jurisdiction, the public
consists of all residents of the city. Thus, disqualification is
required unless the redevelopment decisions will affect Ms.

File No. A-91-054
Page 7


Stanton's economic interests in substantially the same manner as
they will affect all residents of the city, or a significant seg-
ment of the residents of the city. Since the Jazzercise corporate
office is located within the redevelopment area, it is highly
unlikely that Jazzercise will be affected in the same manner as
the public generally. (See In re Legan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1, copy
enclosed.)

Ms. Stanton will also have to examine the effect of the
second redevelopment project on her personal residence to
ascertain if she will be affected in a manner distinguishable from
the public generally.

I trust this answers your question. If you have any further
questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel


By: Jill R) Stecher
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JRS:dg

Enclosure

QUESTION: Under the Act, may ljts. Stanton participate in certain
projects pending before the redeveloPnent commission?
CONCLUS TON
Ms. Stanton may participate in projects pending before the
redevelopnent cornnission if there wiIl. not be a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on either Jazzercise, Inc.
or on her real property interest.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission April 22, 1991 Vincent F. Biondo, Jr. City Attorney City of Carlsbad 1200 Carlsbad Village Drive Carlsbad, CA 92008-1989 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-91-054 Dear Mr. Biondo: You have requested advice concerning application of the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") to the duties of Carlsbad City Councilmember Margaret Stanton. She is also a member of the Carlsbad Redevelopment Com- mission. The following advice is based upon the facts provided in your letter and our telephone conversation on March 22, 1991. QUESTION Under the Act, may Ms. Stanton participate in certain projects pending before the redevelopment commission? CONCLUSION Ms. Stanton may participate in projects pending before the redevelopment commission if there will not be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on either Jazzercise, Inc. or on her real property interest. FACTS Ms. Stanton is an officer and director of Jazzercise, Inc. She is the general manager of the company and an employee of the corporation. She does not have any ownership interest in the 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 File No. A-91-054 Page 2 corporation, which is closely-held and wholly-owned by the Missett Family Trust. Jazzercise operates approximately 4,500 franchises worldwide. Its revenues from the franchises, a mail order and retail store and its television operation are approximately $15 million annu- ally. You stated that the pretax income of Jazzercise is ap- proximately $1 million, the net income exceeds $400,000 and the tangible assets are approximately $2 million. The corporate office is located at 2808 Roosevelt Street, which is in the redevelopment area of Carlsbad. The corporation signed a five year lease for this office in October 1990. The corporation operates a mail order and retail store and a televi- sion production company at this location. It also subleases a part of the offices to several of its local franchisees for dance fitness classes. These franchisees pay Jazzercise 35 percent of the gross student fees collected per month as rent and 20 percent of the gross student fees collected as a continuing franchise fee. The City of Carlsbad adopted a redevelopment plan in 1981. It includes six subareas with specific goals and desired land uses. The Jazzercise corporate offices are located within subarea 5, which is planned as the major tourist and related commercial center for the project area. The following redevelopment projects are pending and will go to the redevelopment commission for a vote in the near future: 1. A parking plan, including financing sources, which identifies how parking needs for the village redevelopment area may be met for future projects. Preparation of the parking plan was funded by the redevelopment tax
